topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. "Regrettably, the surprise celebration for Sarah is cancelled." I reread the message three times, my thumb hovering over the 'reply' button before letting my hand drop. The party was next Saturday. Everyone had been coordinating for weeks, the secret meticulously guarded. Now, this abrupt notification felt like a poorly executed prank. I glanced at the calendar, then at the hastily scribbled notes pinned to the corkboard in the living room—notes detailing decorations, food assignments, and secret guest lists. A knot tightened in my stomach, cold and hard. I walked over to the phone, my fingers tracing the screen as if expecting a hidden message to appear. I dialed Sarah’s number, my voice unnaturally level. "Hey, Sarah? Everything okay?" Her response was brief, almost too cheerful. "Yeah, just a bit busy. What’s up?" I waited, listening intently to the cadence of her tone, searching for any flicker of the usual excitement I expected.

[story 2]

I found the cancellation notice tucked between a utility bill and a junk mail flyer. It was printed on thick, official-looking paper, which made the casual nature of the announcement all the more jarring. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I started pacing the length of the kitchen, the floorboards cool beneath my bare feet. I picked up my phone, intending to call the organizer, but hesitated. What if it was just a mistake? I started rifling through my own messages, scanning the threads about the venue booking and the catering confirmations. Every detail seemed perfectly placed, too neat. I watched my reflection in the darkened window; my eyes felt too wide, my breathing shallow. I muttered to myself, "This can't be right," the words sounding brittle in the quiet apartment. I picked up a pen and began aggressively crossing out sections of the planning sheet, a frantic need to verify the reality of the situation overwhelming me.

[story 3]

When the text message finally arrived, it was delivered with an air of finality. No explanation, just the cancellation. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the device. A strange, hollow sensation washed over me, like a sudden drop in temperature. I didn't move for a full minute, just absorbing the silence in the room, which suddenly felt amplified and heavy. I looked around the apartment, cataloging the familiar objects—the half-inflated balloon, the string of fairy lights draped over the bookshelf—and they seemed suddenly staged, like props left on a set that had been abruptly shut down. I took a deep, shaky breath, forcing myself to rationalize it. Maybe it was a scheduling conflict. But the feeling persisted, a persistent, low hum of dissonance beneath my skin that refused to settle. I stood up abruptly, needing movement, and began systematically checking the door locks, a nervous habit taking over.
